explicit_text: I was born at home in Pasadena, California, and it was where I grew up before the war. My father worked as a gardener there, and my family lived in a rented house with a kind landlord behind us. Pasadena was where I went to school and spent my childhood summers going to the beach with my family. It was a place filled with memories of my early life and the community that supported us before everything changed.
implicit_text: I was born at home in a city in Southern California, and it was where I grew up before the war. My father worked as a gardener there, and my family lived in a rented house with a kind landlord behind us. This city was where I went to school and spent my childhood summers going to the beach with my family. It was a place filled with memories of my early life and the community that supported us before everything changed.

explicit_text: During the summers before the war, my family and I would go to Redondo Beach to set up a big tent and enjoy the beach. It was one of the few beaches we were allowed to visit, along with the bay in San Pedro. These beach trips were a special part of my childhood weekends and family time. Redondo Beach holds those fond memories of simpler days before the war disrupted our lives.
implicit_text: During the summers before the war, my family and I would go to a coastal city in California to set up a big tent and enjoy the beach. It was one of the few beaches we were allowed to visit, along with the bay area in a nearby port city. These beach trips were a special part of my childhood weekends and family time. That beach town holds those fond memories of simpler days before the war disrupted our lives.

explicit_text: San Pedro was another place we visited on weekends, especially to see my uncles who lived there. My uncle, who was in the Japanese Army, lived in San Pedro in a large three-story house before he was taken by the FBI. It was a seaside town, and my family had strong ties there. San Pedro was part of my family's life and history before the war changed everything.
implicit_text: We often visited a port town in Los Angeles on weekends, especially to see my uncles who lived there. My uncle, who was in the Japanese Army, lived in a large three-story house in that town before he was taken by federal agents. It was a seaside community, and my family had strong ties there. That town was part of my family's life and history before the war changed everything.

explicit_text: Both my parents were Issei from Ehime Prefecture on the island of Shikoku in Japan. They came from charming seaside towns there, and even after coming to the U.S., they wanted to be buried in Japan. I have visited their graves in Ehime, which connects me to my family's origins and heritage. Their roots in Ehime shaped much of our family's identity and traditions.
implicit_text: Both my parents were first-generation immigrants from a prefecture on the island of Shikoku in Japan. They came from charming seaside towns there, and even after coming to the U.S., they wanted to be buried in their homeland. I have visited their graves in that prefecture, which connects me to my family's origins and heritage. Their roots there shaped much of our family's identity and traditions.

explicit_text: Shikoku is the island in Japan where my parents came from, specifically Ehime Prefecture. It was a place of their youth and family life before they immigrated to the U.S. My parents' connection to Shikoku remained strong, and they wished to be buried there. This island represents the homeland that my family left behind but always remembered.
implicit_text: The island in Japan where my parents came from is where their youth and family life were centered before they immigrated to the U.S. My parents' connection to this island remained strong, and they wished to be buried there. This island represents the homeland that my family left behind but always remembered.

explicit_text: My father first came to the U.S. in 1919 and worked in Spokane, Washington, on the railroads. He earned enough money there to return to Japan and marry my mother in an arranged marriage. Spokane was the place where my father started his American journey before settling in California. It was an important chapter in our family's immigration story.
implicit_text: My father first came to the U.S. in 1919 and worked on the railroads in a city in Washington state. He earned enough money there to return to Japan and marry my mother in an arranged marriage. That city was the place where my father started his American journey before settling in California. It was an important chapter in our family's immigration story.

explicit_text: Before the war, my family and I would go to the Zen temple in Little Tokyo, Los Angeles, where we attended Japanese school. Little Tokyo was a cultural center for us and a place where we connected with our heritage. I also remember passing through Union Station in Los Angeles, near Little Tokyo, when we were later sent to the relocation center. Little Tokyo was a familiar and important part of my early life.
implicit_text: Before the war, my family and I would go to the Zen temple in the Japanese American district of Los Angeles, where we attended Japanese school. That neighborhood was a cultural center for us and a place where we connected with our heritage. I also remember passing through the main train station in Los Angeles, near that district, when we were later sent to the relocation center. That area was a familiar and important part of my early life.

explicit_text: I don't remember the exact moment Pearl Harbor was attacked, but my father talked about it a lot. On December 7, 1941, my sister told my father about the bombing, and at first, he couldn't believe it because he thought Japan wouldn't do such a thing. He was shocked and felt the people in the Navy understood the world better than the Army did. The attack brought a sense of foreboding that our lives were about to change drastically.
implicit_text: I don't remember the exact moment the naval base in Hawaii was attacked, but my father talked about it a lot. On December 7, 1941, my sister told my father about the bombing, and at first, he couldn't believe it because he thought Japan wouldn't do such a thing. He was shocked and felt the people in the Navy understood the world better than the Army did. The attack brought a sense of foreboding that our lives were about to change drastically.

explicit_text: My father believed that the people in the Imperial Japanese Navy had a better understanding of the world and would not have supported the attack on Pearl Harbor. He was shocked by the attack because he thought Japan was too small to take on a country as large as America. His respect for the Navy's perspective made him doubt the Army's actions. This distinction shaped how my family processed the news of the war.
implicit_text: My father believed that the people in the Japanese naval forces had a better understanding of the world and would not have supported the attack on the naval base in Hawaii. He was shocked by the attack because he thought Japan was too small to take on a country as large as America. His respect for the Navy's perspective made him doubt the Army's actions. This distinction shaped how my family processed the news of the war.

explicit_text: When Executive Order 9066 was issued, my family knew something was coming. My mother started sewing duffle bags and heavy bathrobes for us because there were rumors we'd be sent to cold or desert places. My uncle had already been taken by the FBI, accused of signaling Japanese ships from his porch in San Pedro. We prepared as best we could, storing our belongings with neighbors and friends, knowing our lives were about to change forever.
implicit_text: When the presidential order authorizing military zones was issued, my family knew something was coming. My mother started sewing duffle bags and heavy bathrobes for us because there were rumors we'd be sent to cold or desert places. My uncle had already been taken by federal agents, accused of signaling Japanese ships from his porch in the port town. We prepared as best we could, storing our belongings with neighbors and friends, knowing our lives were about to change forever.

explicit_text: My mother's brother was in the Japanese Army, and we had pictures of him that were burned when the FBI came through our albums. My uncle, living in San Pedro, was taken away because the FBI accused him of signaling Japanese ships. The presence of family in the Japanese Army made the situation even more tense and frightening for us. It was a painful reminder of how the war impacted our family directly.
implicit_text: My mother's brother was in the Japanese Army, and we had pictures of him that were burned when federal agents came through our albums. My uncle, living in the port town, was taken away because the agents accused him of signaling Japanese ships. The presence of family in the Japanese Army made the situation even more tense and frightening for us. It was a painful reminder of how the war impacted our family directly.

explicit_text: The FBI took my uncle away from his home in San Pedro, accusing him of signaling Japanese ships from his porch. This was before the mass relocation began, and it was a frightening sign that our family was under suspicion. The FBI's actions contributed to the fear and uncertainty we felt as a family. Their involvement marked the beginning of the upheaval in our lives during the war.
implicit_text: Federal agents took my uncle away from his home in the port town, accusing him of signaling Japanese ships from his porch. explicit_text: Our first stop after leaving Pasadena was the assembly center in Tulare, California, which was a fairground next to Highway 99. We lived in barracks there, which was better than the horse stalls some families had to endure. I remember the fear of the fence and the warning that if we got too close, they might shoot us. The assembly center was a strange and frightening place where I got very sick after receiving vaccinations.
implicit_text: Our first stop after leaving the Southern California city was the assembly center in a Central California town, which was a fairground next to a major highway. We lived in barracks there, which was better than the horse stalls some families had to endure. I remember the fear of the fence and the warning that if we got too close, they might shoot us. The assembly center was a strange and frightening place where I got very sick after receiving vaccinations.

explicit_text: The Tulare assembly center where we were held was right next to Highway 99, and I remember watching cars whiz by while we were fenced in. It was a constant reminder of the freedom we had lost. The highway was visible from the camp, but we were trapped behind barbed wire. That contrast made the experience even more surreal and difficult for me as a child.
implicit_text: The assembly center where we were held was right next to a major California highway, and I remember watching cars whiz by while we were fenced in. It was a constant reminder of the freedom we had lost. The highway was visible from the camp, but we were trapped behind barbed wire. That contrast made the experience even more surreal and difficult for me as a child.

explicit_text: In September, we left Tulare and went to the Gila River War Relocation Center in Arizona. The camp was in a desert area near Phoenix, and when we arrived, it wasn’t quite ready yet. I remember crossing planks over open ditches to get to the bathroom, which scared me so much I used a chamber pot instead. Life at Gila River was hard, but we tried to make the best of it, and I even went back to school there with my cousin as my teacher.
implicit_text: In September, we left the assembly center and went to a war relocation center in Arizona. The camp was in a desert area near the state's capital, and when we arrived, it wasn’t quite ready yet. I remember crossing planks over open ditches to get to the bathroom, which scared me so much I used a chamber pot instead. Life at the desert camp was hard, but we tried to make the best of it, and I even went back to school there with my cousin as my teacher.

explicit_text: When we were transported from Tulare to Gila River, we stopped at Union Station in Los Angeles. I knew the station well from trips with my family to Little Tokyo, but this time we had to keep the train shades down because soldiers with rifles were patrolling. I wanted to peek out but was too scared to. Union Station was a familiar place that took on a very different meaning during our forced relocation.

I wanted to peek out but was too scared to. That station was a familiar place that took on a very different meaning during our forced relocation.

explicit_text: After arriving at Union Station, we were taken to Phoenix, Arizona, before reaching the Gila River camp. Back then, Phoenix was a desolate desert area, very different from the city it is today. We didn’t know where we were going or what to expect, and the long ride through the desert was frightening. Phoenix was the gateway to the harsh new reality we faced at the relocation center.

explicit_text: My cousin, who was a sophomore at the University of Chicago before being sent to the camp, became my second grade teacher at Gila River. She prepared lesson plans and flashcards, and I studied with her, which helped me catch up on school. Having her as my teacher made school a bright spot in the difficult camp life. The University of Chicago connection was a source of pride and encouragement for me during that time.

The connection to that university was a source of pride and encouragement for me during that time.

explicit_text: Eleanor Roosevelt was mentioned in conversations about the war and the treatment of Japanese Americans, symbolizing hope and leadership during difficult times. Although I did not have a direct encounter with her, her name came up as someone who cared about the plight of people like us. She represented a figure of compassion and understanding in a time of widespread fear and discrimination.
